nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.label: AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.excerpt: AI agents use models to choose actions, call tools, observe results, and continue toward a goal. Tool use turns an LLM from a text generator into a system that can retrieve data, execute operations, and affect external state.

nodes.label: AI Alignment Overview
nodes.excerpt: AI alignment is the problem of making AI systems pursue goals and behaviors that match human intent, values, and safety constraints. It includes instruction following, truthfulness, robustness, oversight, and control.

nodes.label: AI Memory Architectures
nodes.excerpt: AI memory architectures organize how agents store, retrieve, update, and use information over time. Useful memory systems combine short-term context, long-term retrieval, summaries, user preferences, and safety controls.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.label: AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.excerpt: AI interpretability research tries to understand what neural networks represent internally and why they produce particular outputs. It supports safety by making model behavior less opaque and easier to audit.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.label: Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.excerpt: Activation functions introduce nonlinear behavior into neural networks and shape how signals flow through layers. ReLU, GELU, sigmoid, tanh, and softmax each serve different roles in modern models.

nodes.label: Attention Mechanism — Self-Attention
nodes.excerpt: Self-attention lets each token in a sequence dynamically read information from other tokens. It is the core mechanism that allows transformers to build contextual representations.

nodes.label: Backpropagation Explained
nodes.excerpt: Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes gradients for neural network parameters efficiently. It applies the chain rule backward through the network so optimizers know how to reduce loss.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.label: Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.excerpt: Batch normalization and layer normalization stabilize neural network training by normalizing intermediate activations. BatchNorm is common in CNNs, while LayerNorm is central to transformers and sequence models.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.label: Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.excerpt: AI benchmarks are standardized tasks used to compare model capabilities. MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, and GPQA test different slices of knowledge, coding, math reasoning, and expert-level question answering.

nodes.label: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
nodes.excerpt: Chain-of-thought prompting encourages a model to work through intermediate reasoning steps before answering. It can improve complex task performance, but production systems often need concise hidden reasoning or structured scratchpads rather than verbose explanations.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.label: Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.excerpt: Chunking strategies decide how documents are split before retrieval in RAG systems. Good chunks preserve enough context to answer questions while staying small enough for precise search and prompt budgets.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.label: Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.excerpt: The Claude API exposes Anthropic's Claude models through a Messages-style interface and official SDKs. Practical use centers on model selection, message structure, max tokens, streaming, tool use, safety, and cost control.

nodes.label: Constitutional AI
nodes.excerpt: Constitutional AI trains models using explicit principles that guide critique, revision, and preference selection. It aims to improve helpfulness and harmlessness while reducing dependence on direct human labeling for every case.

nodes.label: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
nodes.excerpt: Convolutional neural networks use learned filters to detect local spatial patterns in images and grid-like data. Their inductive bias makes them efficient for vision tasks such as classification, detection, and segmentation.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.label: Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Cosine similarity and distance metrics define how vector representations are compared. The metric choice affects semantic search, clustering, recommendations, and nearest-neighbor retrieval quality.

nodes.label: Diffusion Models
nodes.excerpt: Diffusion models generate data by learning to reverse a gradual noising process. They are widely used for image, audio, and video generation because they produce high-quality samples with controllable conditioning.

nodes.label: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
nodes.excerpt: Direct Preference Optimization trains language models directly from preference pairs without fitting a separate reward model and running an RL loop. It is a simpler alternative to classic RLHF for many alignment workflows.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.label: Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.excerpt: Emergent abilities are capabilities that appear suddenly or become measurable only after models reach sufficient scale. The concept is debated because some emergence reflects real phase changes, while some reflects metric and evaluation artifacts.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.label: Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.excerpt: Few-shot and zero-shot learning describe a model's ability to perform tasks with little or no task-specific training data. In LLMs, these capabilities often come from prompting rather than updating model weights.

nodes.label: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
nodes.excerpt: Generative Adversarial Networks train a generator and discriminator in competition. GANs can create sharp samples but are difficult to train and have largely been displaced by diffusion models for many image-generation tasks.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.label: Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.excerpt: Gradient descent variants are optimization algorithms that update model parameters using gradients. SGD, AdaGrad, and Adam differ in how they use noise, learning history, momentum, and per-parameter step sizes.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.label: Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.excerpt: Hallucination is when a model produces unsupported, false, or fabricated output. It comes from next-token generation, incomplete context, weak uncertainty calibration, and objective mismatch, so mitigation requires retrieval, constraints, verification, and good UX.

nodes.label: Knowledge Distillation
nodes.excerpt: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to imitate a larger or stronger teacher model. It can reduce inference cost while preserving much of the teacher's behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.label: Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.excerpt: Local LLMs run open-weight language models on personal or self-managed hardware using tools such as Ollama, llama.cpp, and LM Studio. They trade frontier-model quality for privacy, offline use, customization, and cost control.

nodes.label: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
nodes.excerpt: Long Short-Term Memory networks are recurrent models with gates that control what information is stored, forgotten, and emitted. They were a major solution to long-range dependency problems before transformers.

nodes.label: Loss Functions
nodes.excerpt: Loss functions define what a model is penalized for during training. They convert prediction errors into a scalar objective that optimization algorithms can minimize.

nodes.label: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
nodes.excerpt: Mixture of Experts models route each input token or example through a subset of specialized expert networks. MoE increases total model capacity while keeping per-token compute lower than a dense model of the same size.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.excerpt: Model Context Protocol is an open protocol for connecting AI applications to external tools and context providers. MCP servers expose capabilities such as tools, resources, and prompts through a standardized interface.

nodes.label: Model Evaluation Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Model evaluation metrics measure whether a model is performing the task that matters. Good evaluation separates training loss from real-world quality, reliability, safety, latency, and cost.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.label: Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.excerpt: Model quantization compresses neural network weights and activations into lower-precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. It reduces memory and inference cost, often with some quality tradeoff.

nodes.label: Multi-Head Attention
nodes.excerpt: Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel so a model can capture different relationships at once. Each head learns its own projections and contributes to the layer output.

nodes.label: Multimodal Models
nodes.excerpt: Multimodal models process and relate more than one data type, such as text, images, audio, video, or actions. They make AI systems more useful because real-world tasks rarely arrive as text alone.

nodes.label: Neural Network Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Neural network fundamentals cover how layered mathematical models learn patterns by adjusting weights from data. The core ideas are neurons, parameters, activations, loss, backpropagation, and gradient-based optimization.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.label: OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.excerpt: The OpenAI API provides access to GPT and specialized models through interfaces such as the Responses API. Practical integration involves model choice, structured outputs, tools, streaming, embeddings, safety identifiers, and cost control.

nodes.label: Positional Encoding
nodes.excerpt: Positional encoding gives transformers information about token order. Because self-attention alone is permutation-insensitive, models need positional signals such as sinusoidal embeddings, learned positions, or RoPE.

nodes.label: Pre-training vs Fine-tuning
nodes.excerpt: Pre-training teaches a model broad statistical representations from large datasets, while fine-tuning adapts that model to narrower tasks or behaviors. The distinction explains why modern ML often starts from foundation models instead of training from scratch.

nodes.label: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Prompt engineering is the practice of structuring instructions, context, examples, and constraints so an LLM produces useful outputs. It is less about magic phrasing and more about clear task specification and feedback loops.

nodes.label: RLHF vs RLAIF
nodes.excerpt: RLHF uses human preference feedback while RLAIF uses AI-generated feedback to train model behavior. Both optimize preference signals, but they differ in cost, scalability, bias, oversight, and failure modes.

nodes.label: ReAct Prompting Pattern
nodes.excerpt: ReAct is a prompting pattern that interleaves reasoning and acting. It helps language-model agents decide what to do, call tools, observe results, and continue with updated context.

nodes.label: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
nodes.excerpt: Recurrent neural networks process sequences by carrying hidden state from one step to the next. They were historically important for language, speech, and time series before transformers became dominant.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.label: Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.excerpt: Regularization techniques reduce overfitting by constraining how a model learns from training data. L1, L2, dropout, data augmentation, and early stopping encourage better generalization.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Reinforcement learning trains agents to choose actions that maximize reward over time. Its core frame is agent, environment, state, action, reward, policy, value, and exploration.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.excerpt: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback trains models toward human-preferred behavior using preference data, reward models, and policy optimization. It is a key method behind instruction-following assistants.

nodes.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
nodes.excerpt: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines search with LLM generation by adding relevant external documents to the prompt. It helps models answer with current or private knowledge without retraining.

nodes.label: Scaling Laws
nodes.excerpt: Scaling laws describe predictable relationships between model performance, parameter count, dataset size, and compute. They help labs allocate training budgets and forecast capability improvements.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.label: Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.excerpt: Semantic search retrieves by meaning using embeddings, while keyword search retrieves by exact or lexical term matching. Strong search systems often combine both because each fails in different ways.

nodes.label: Speech Recognition (Whisper, Wav2Vec)
nodes.excerpt: Speech recognition converts audio into text using acoustic modeling, sequence modeling, and language context. Whisper and Wav2Vec represent modern neural approaches that learn robust audio representations.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.label: Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.excerpt: Text-to-speech models convert text into natural-sounding audio. Modern TTS systems model linguistic content, speaker identity, prosody, timing, and waveform generation.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.label: Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.excerpt: Tokenization converts raw text into model-readable token IDs. BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece use subword units so models can handle large vocabularies, rare words, and multilingual text.

nodes.label: Transfer Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer learning reuses knowledge learned on one dataset or task to improve performance on another. It is the reason pre-trained models can be adapted efficiently to new domains with less data.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.label: Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Transformer architecture uses self-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, normalization, and positional information to model sequences in parallel. It is the foundation of modern large language models.

nodes.label: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
nodes.excerpt: Variational Autoencoders learn a structured latent space by encoding data into probability distributions and decoding samples back into data. They are useful for representation learning, generation, and latent compression.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.label: Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.excerpt: Vector databases store embeddings and support fast nearest-neighbor search with metadata filtering. They are commonly used for semantic search, recommendations, and RAG systems.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.label: Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.excerpt: Vector embeddings represent items such as text, images, users, or products as numerical vectors where distance captures semantic similarity. They make semantic search, clustering, recommendations, and RAG retrieval possible.

nodes.label: Vision-Language Models
nodes.excerpt: Vision-language models connect visual inputs with language understanding and generation. They support image captioning, visual question answering, document understanding, UI analysis, and multimodal agents.

nodes.label: AI Workflow
nodes.excerpt: multi-model AI workflow: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, IDE Copilot routing guide.

nodes.excerpt: capability benchmarks (SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, OSWorld), safety eval (HarmBench, jailbreak taxonomy, dangerous capability evals), deployment monitoring, A/B testing, evaluation flywheel.

nodes.label: Agent Memory

nodes.label: Agent Security
nodes.excerpt: security model for tool-using AI agents: prompt injection, capability-based tools, least privilege, sandboxing, memory poisoning, policy engines, audit logs, and Bobby permission modes.

nodes.excerpt: ChromaDB + SQLite + Obsidian vault three-layer memory system.

nodes.label: Caching
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: cache layers, read/write strategies, eviction policies, stampede/penetration/avalanche problems, Redis internals.

nodes.label: Claude Code Skills
nodes.excerpt: how Claude Code skills work: markdown files injected into context, gstack architecture (templates + Bun build), gbrain per-project learnings, how to write your own skill.

nodes.label: Cloud Cost Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot, right-sizing, Graviton, auto scaling patterns, S3 lifecycle, NAT Gateway mitigation, data transfer costs, tagging, Cost Explorer.

nodes.label: Cloud Networking

nodes.label: Cloud Services
nodes.excerpt: AWS and GCP services: EC2/GCE, Lambda/Cloud Functions, S3/GCS, EBS, RDS/Aurora, DynamoDB/Firestore, ElastiCache, SQS/SNS/Pub-Sub, ECS/EKS/GKE/Cloud Run, CloudFront, ALB/NLB, IAM, CloudWatch.

nodes.label: DSA Practice
nodes.excerpt: DSA patterns by category (arrays, trees, graphs, DP, backtracking, binary search) with spaced repetition notes.

nodes.label: Data Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ELT vs ETL, data lake (Parquet, Iceberg, Medallion architecture), dbt, Airflow/Dagster, Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift, Spark, Flink, Kinesis, Glue, CDC/Debezium, reverse ETL.

nodes.label: A-B Testing Design
nodes.excerpt: A/B testing compares product or system variants using randomized assignment and statistical analysis. Good test design defines the hypothesis, unit of randomization, metrics, sample size, guardrails, and stopping rules before launch.

nodes.label: Anomaly Detection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Anomaly detection finds observations or patterns that differ meaningfully from expected behavior. Useful methods range from simple thresholds to statistical baselines, clustering, isolation forests, and time-series models.

nodes.label: Bayesian Statistics
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian statistics treats probability as a degree of belief updated by evidence. It combines prior beliefs with observed data to produce posterior beliefs.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.label: Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.excerpt: Causal inference asks whether an action or exposure caused an outcome, not merely whether two variables moved together. It uses experiments, assumptions, controls, and quasi-experimental methods to reason about cause and effect.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.label: Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.excerpt: Clustering groups unlabeled data points by similarity. K-means, DBSCAN, and hierarchical clustering each make different assumptions about cluster shape, density, and how many groups exist.

nodes.label: Cohort Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Cohort analysis groups users or entities by a shared start condition and tracks behavior over time. It is especially useful for retention, activation, lifecycle, and product-quality questions.

nodes.label: Confidence Intervals
nodes.excerpt: Confidence intervals estimate a plausible range for an unknown population value from sample data. They communicate uncertainty better than a single point estimate, but they are often misread as probabilities about one fixed interval.

nodes.label: Dashboard Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Dashboard design organizes metrics so users can monitor status, diagnose changes, and act. Effective dashboards prioritize audience, metric definitions, hierarchy, freshness, comparisons, and drill-down paths.

nodes.label: Data Cleaning Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Data cleaning turns messy, inconsistent, duplicated, or invalid data into a reliable analysis dataset. It is less glamorous than modeling but often determines whether conclusions are trustworthy.

nodes.label: Data Lake Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Data lake architecture stores raw and processed data in scalable object storage for analytics, ML, and archival use. Its value depends on metadata, governance, file formats, partitioning, and transformation discipline.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.label: Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.excerpt: Data normalization and scaling transform numeric features into comparable ranges or distributions. They are essential for distance-based models, gradient-based optimization, and stable feature interpretation.

nodes.label: Data Warehousing Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Data warehousing organizes cleaned, queryable data for analytics and reporting. Core concepts include facts, dimensions, star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, marts, lineage, and governance.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.label: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.excerpt: Dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions for visualization, denoising, or modeling. PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP differ in whether they preserve global variance, local neighborhoods, or manifold structure.

nodes.label: ETL vs ELT
nodes.excerpt: ETL transforms data before loading it into a target store, while ELT loads raw data first and transforms it inside the warehouse or lakehouse. The choice affects governance, cost, flexibility, and pipeline ownership.

nodes.label: Feature Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Feature engineering turns raw data into model inputs that expose useful signal. It often matters more than model choice because features encode domain knowledge, time context, aggregations, and constraints.

nodes.label: Feature Selection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Feature selection chooses which variables to keep for modeling. It improves interpretability, reduces noise, lowers cost, and can prevent overfitting when done with proper validation.

nodes.label: Funnel Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Funnel analysis measures how users move through a sequence of steps toward a goal. It identifies drop-off points, conversion rates, and where product or process changes may matter most.

nodes.label: Handling Missing Data
nodes.excerpt: Handling missing data means understanding why values are absent and choosing a treatment that preserves valid inference. Deletion, imputation, missingness indicators, and model-aware methods each carry assumptions.

nodes.label: Hypothesis Testing
nodes.excerpt: Hypothesis testing evaluates whether observed data is surprising under a null hypothesis. It is useful for experiments, but it depends heavily on assumptions, sample size, and careful interpretation.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.label: Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.excerpt: Choosing metrics and KPIs means deciding what behavior the team should optimize and monitor. Strong metrics are aligned with goals, hard to game, clearly defined, and paired with guardrails.

nodes.label: OLAP vs OLTP
nodes.excerpt: OLTP systems handle live transactions, while OLAP systems support analytical queries over large historical datasets. Confusing the two leads to slow apps, expensive queries, and unreliable reporting.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.label: P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.excerpt: P-values measure how surprising observed data would be under a null hypothesis. Statistical significance is a thresholding convention, not proof that an effect is real, large, or important.

nodes.label: Probability Theory Basics
nodes.excerpt: Probability theory models uncertainty using events, random variables, distributions, expectation, and conditional probability. It underpins statistics, machine learning, experimentation, and risk reasoning.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.label: Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.excerpt: Regression analysis models relationships between inputs and an outcome. Linear, logistic, and polynomial regression are foundational tools for prediction, explanation, and estimating effects with assumptions.

nodes.label: SQL Window Functions
nodes.excerpt: SQL window functions compute values across related rows without collapsing the result set. They are essential for rankings, running totals, moving averages, retention analysis, and event-sequence queries.

nodes.label: SQL for Analytics
nodes.excerpt: SQL for analytics uses relational queries to answer business, product, and operational questions. The practical skill is joining, filtering, grouping, aggregating, and validating data without losing the meaning of the metric.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.label: Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.excerpt: Statistics fundamentals summarize data with measures of center and spread. Mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation are the starting vocabulary for reasoning about datasets.

nodes.label: Survival Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Survival analysis models time until an event occurs, such as churn, failure, conversion, or relapse. It handles censored data where the event has not happened yet during the observation window.

nodes.label: Time Series Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Time series analysis studies data ordered over time, such as metrics, demand, latency, or prices. It focuses on trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, forecasting, and anomaly detection.

nodes.label: Visualization Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Visualization best practices help charts communicate patterns without distorting the data. Good visualizations choose the right chart type, scale, labeling, ordering, color, and context for the question.

nodes.label: dbt (Data Build Tool)
nodes.excerpt: dbt is a transformation tool that lets analytics teams build versioned, tested SQL models in the warehouse. It brings software engineering practices such as modularity, lineage, documentation, and CI to analytics work.

nodes.label: Data Structures and Algorithms Patterns
nodes.excerpt: LeetCode/NeetCode pattern guide covering arrays, hash maps, two pointers, sliding windows, heaps, graphs, union find, backtracking, DP, and tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Internals Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper database internals: pages, WAL, MVCC, vacuum/bloat, indexes, query planner, isolation anomalies, locks, replication, backups, partitioning, distributed SQL.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: ACID, MVCC, indexes (B-tree/hash/composite/covering), query planning, N+1, connection pooling, SQL vs all 4 NoSQL types, PostgreSQL specifics.

nodes.label: Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: partial failures, CAP/PACELC, consistency models, replication (single-leader/multi-leader/leaderless), Raft consensus, 2PC vs Saga, vector clocks, gossip protocol.

nodes.label: Docker
nodes.excerpt: OverlayFS layer system, Dockerfile best practices (multi-stage builds), networking (bridge/host), volumes, container runtime architecture (dockerd→containerd→runc→kernel).

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: focused DP guide covering state design, memoization vs tabulation, 1D/2D DP, knapsack, LIS, interval DP, tree DP, bitmask DP, common traps, and practice method.

nodes.label: Economics and Personal Finance
nodes.excerpt: practical economics and personal finance primer covering incentives, opportunity cost, supply/demand, compounding, emergency funds, debt, index funds, retirement accounts, taxes, credit, lifestyle inflation, and career finance.

nodes.label: Engineering Judgment and Tool Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: practical guide to choosing tools by fit, failure modes, accepted tradeoffs, monitoring signals, and simpler alternatives.

nodes.label: FlashAttention
nodes.excerpt: GPU memory hierarchy, why standard attention is HBM-bound, tiling algorithm, online softmax, FA-2 and FA-3.

nodes.label: Formal Methods Agent Safety
nodes.excerpt: temporal logic specs, type systems, runtime monitors, NN verification (and its limits), probabilistic verification, neurosymbolic approaches.

nodes.label: Geospatial
nodes.excerpt: geohashing (8-neighbor fix), quadtrees, S2/Hilbert curve, Haversine formula, Yelp-like proximity design, Uber-like live driver matching with Redis GEOSEARCH, H3 hexagons, map tile systems.

nodes.label: IaaS PaaS SaaS
nodes.excerpt: service model spectrum: IaaS (raw VMs), PaaS (managed runtime), SaaS (fully managed app), FaaS (functions), and where the lines blur.

nodes.label: Interview Communication
nodes.excerpt: interview communication guide covering project explanations, STAR stories, coding/system design structure, debugging out loud, clarifying questions, recovery, and resume bullet framing.

nodes.label: Kubernetes
nodes.excerpt: desired state reconciliation, control plane, worker nodes, all core objects (Pod/Deployment/Service/Ingress/StatefulSet/HPA/PV), scheduling, health probes, networking (CNI/kube-proxy/CoreDNS), RBAC.

nodes.label: LLM Capabilities and AI Ethics
nodes.excerpt: opinionated overview of what LLMs can and cannot do, their tradeoffs, whether they replace engineers/humans, and the philosophy, morality, and ethics of AI deployment.

nodes.label: LLM System Design
nodes.excerpt: tokens, context windows, prompt caching (KV cache, TTL, cost math), streaming, tool use/agentic loops, RAG (chunking, hybrid search, reranking), multi-agent orchestration, evals, fine-tuning vs prompting.

nodes.label: MLOps and ML Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ML lifecycle from data and labels through training, experiment tracking, model registry, deployment, monitoring, drift, retraining, and LLMOps for Bobby-like systems.

nodes.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
nodes.excerpt: reverse-engineering transformers: circuit hypothesis, induction heads, IOI circuit, superposition, sparse autoencoders, activation patching.

nodes.label: Message Queues
nodes.excerpt: delivery guarantees (at-most/at-least/exactly-once), Kafka internals (partitions, offsets, consumer groups, zero-copy, EOS), SQS, RabbitMQ, outbox pattern, event sourcing, backpressure.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent RL
nodes.excerpt: MARL: non-stationarity, CTDE, QMIX monotonicity, MAPPO, self-play, league play, emergent communication, credit assignment.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.excerpt: multi-agent architectures (orchestrator-worker, blackboard, peer-to-peer), emergent behaviors, failure modes, safety and control.

nodes.label: Networking
nodes.excerpt: OSI model, IP/NAT/CIDR, TCP internals (handshake, sliding window, congestion control, BBR), UDP, DNS resolution, TLS 1.3 (Diffie-Hellman, forward secrecy, mTLS), HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 (QUIC).

nodes.label: Notification System Design
nodes.excerpt: End-to-end notification system design covering aggregation, two-tier pipelines, reliable delivery, the outbox pattern, and a comparison against Meta's production system.

nodes.label: OS Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: processes (fork/exec/CoW), virtual memory (page tables, MMU, TLB), file descriptors, I/O models (epoll, io_uring), concurrency primitives, CFS scheduling, signals, namespaces, cgroups.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.excerpt: monitoring vs observability, structured logging, log levels, metrics (RED/USE methods, Prometheus, PromQL), distributed tracing (spans, context propagation, sampling), OpenTelemetry, SLOs/error budgets.

nodes.label: Observability Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: production debugging guide using logs, metrics, traces, SLOs, alerts, postmortems, and case studies for latency, queues, databases, and error spikes.

nodes.label: Process Reward Models
nodes.excerpt: step-level rewards vs outcome rewards: credit assignment, PRM800K, Monte Carlo estimation, test-time compute scaling via MCTS.

nodes.label: Product Engineering
nodes.excerpt: product engineering guide covering problem framing, MVPs, activation, retention, engagement, funnels, user research, prioritization, onboarding, analytics, and project-specific product risks.

nodes.label: Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: When to use each programming language and why — covering the core mental model (control vs. convenience, performance predictability), plus deep reasoning for every major language from C to COBOL, organized by domain.

nodes.label: RAG and Bobby Vector Search
nodes.excerpt: what RAG is, how Bobby's current index-based retrieval works, and when to upgrade to vector search (~300 notes trigger).

nodes.label: RLHF and Alignment
nodes.excerpt: aligning LLMs: SFT, reward modeling, PPO, DPO (with math), Constitutional AI, steering vectors, reward hacking cases, alignment tax.

nodes.label: Rate Limiting
nodes.excerpt: five algorithms (token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window log, sliding window counter), distributed enforcement with Redis, where to enforce, rate limit headers.

nodes.label: VanossGaming - Dewit
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Tyler says \"Dewit\" at 0:53, meaning \"Do it.\".

nodes.id: Resources/References/VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.label: VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Nogla slurs \"bow and arrow\" into \"boner\" at 3:13 while Evan trolls him invisibly.

nodes.label: Scaling
nodes.excerpt: vertical vs. horizontal scaling, sharding strategies (range/hash/consistent hashing/directory), cross-shard queries, replication, and tail latency mitigation (hedged requests, LIFO queues, load shedding).

nodes.label: Search Systems
nodes.excerpt: inverted index, text processing pipeline, TF-IDF vs BM25, Elasticsearch internals (shards, segments, near-real-time write path), autocomplete (trie, edge n-grams, fuzzy), vector search (HNSW), hybrid search with RRF.

nodes.label: Security
nodes.excerpt: threat modeling, authn vs authz, session auth, JWT (alg:none attack, refresh rotation), bcrypt/Argon2, OWASP Top 10, XSS/CSRF/SSRF/race conditions, secrets management, security headers.

nodes.label: Security Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper security guide covering OAuth/OIDC, sessions vs JWTs, passkeys, XSS/CSRF/SSRF, SQL injection, supply chain, container/Kubernetes security, secrets, threat modeling, and incident response.

nodes.label: Software Architecture
nodes.excerpt: architecture guide covering layered, hexagonal, clean architecture, DDD, modular monoliths, microservices, event-driven design, CQRS, dependency inversion, and abstraction tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Software Engineering Tools
nodes.excerpt: full survey of tools modern engineering teams use: design (Figma), IDEs (VS Code, Cursor, Zed), AI assistants (Copilot, Claude Code), CI/CD, observability, databases, and communication tools.

nodes.label: 12-Factor App Methodology
nodes.excerpt: The 12-factor app methodology is a set of practices for building portable, deployable, cloud-friendly services. It emphasizes declarative config, stateless processes, clean dependencies, logs as streams, and strict separation between build and run.

nodes.label: API Design — GraphQL
nodes.excerpt: GraphQL is an API query language and runtime that lets clients request exactly the data shape they need. It is strongest for product surfaces that aggregate many backend entities, but it requires discipline around schema design, authorization, and query cost control.

nodes.label: API Design — REST Principles
nodes.excerpt: REST API design uses HTTP resources, methods, status codes, and representations to expose predictable service interfaces. Good REST design is less about purity and more about stable contracts, clear semantics, and operational usability.

nodes.label: API Design — Server-Sent Events
nodes.excerpt: Server-Sent Events are a simple HTTP-based way for servers to stream updates to clients. SSE is a good fit for one-way live updates where WebSockets would add unnecessary connection and protocol complexity.

nodes.label: API Design — WebSockets
nodes.excerpt: WebSockets provide a persistent bidirectional connection between client and server. They are useful for low-latency interactive features, but they require explicit handling for connection state, scaling, authentication, and backpressure.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.label: API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.excerpt: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework that uses Protocol Buffers to define typed service contracts. It is commonly used for internal service communication where latency, streaming, and strongly typed generated clients matter.

nodes.label: Anti-Patterns and Code Smells
nodes.excerpt: Anti-patterns and code smells are warning signs that a design may be harder to understand, test, or change than it needs to be. They are not automatic proof of bad code, but they are prompts for closer inspection.

nodes.label: Backward Compatibility Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Backward compatibility lets existing clients continue working as systems evolve. It requires additive changes, deprecation windows, schema evolution discipline, and careful deployment ordering.

nodes.label: Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)
nodes.excerpt: Behavior-Driven Development extends test-first development by describing system behavior in language shared by engineering, product, and domain stakeholders. It is most useful when examples clarify requirements across roles.

nodes.label: Blue-Green Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Blue-green deployment keeps two production environments so traffic can switch quickly between old and new versions. It reduces release risk when environments are truly equivalent and rollback is safe.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.label: Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.excerpt: Build systems turn source code into tested, packaged, runnable artifacts. Tools like Make, Bazel, and Nx differ in how much structure they provide for dependency graphs, caching, reproducibility, and monorepo scale.

nodes.label: CQRS Pattern
nodes.excerpt: CQRS separates commands that change state from queries that read state. It is useful when write models and read models have different complexity, performance, or scaling needs.

nodes.label: Canary Releases
nodes.excerpt: Canary releases expose a new version to a small subset of traffic before full rollout. They reduce blast radius and use real production signals to decide whether to proceed.

nodes.label: Chaos Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Chaos engineering deliberately injects controlled failures to test whether systems remain resilient. It turns reliability assumptions into experiments before real incidents expose them.

nodes.label: Clean Code Principles
nodes.excerpt: Clean code principles explain how to write software that other engineers can safely read, change, and extend. The practical focus is clarity, intention-revealing structure, and reducing surprise rather than chasing aesthetic purity.

nodes.label: Code Complexity — Cyclomatic Complexity
nodes.excerpt: Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of independent paths through code. It is a useful warning signal for testing and maintainability, but it should be interpreted with context rather than treated as a universal score.

nodes.label: Code Review Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Code review is a collaborative quality gate for correctness, maintainability, security, and shared understanding. Strong reviews focus on risk and clarity rather than personal preference.

nodes.label: Container Networking
nodes.excerpt: Container networking connects isolated container processes to hosts, other containers, services, and external networks. Understanding bridges, overlays, ports, DNS, and network policies is essential for debugging containerized systems.

nodes.label: Conventional Commits
nodes.excerpt: Conventional Commits is a commit-message convention that adds machine-readable intent to project history. It supports changelog generation, semantic versioning, and easier review of change purpose.

nodes.label: Dependency Management
nodes.excerpt: Dependency management controls how projects select, version, update, audit, and isolate third-party or internal packages. It affects security, reliability, reproducibility, and long-term maintenance.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.excerpt: Behavioral design patterns organize runtime interaction between objects. They help separate algorithms, events, state transitions, commands, and traversal logic from the objects that use them.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.excerpt: Creational design patterns centralize object construction so callers do not need to know every setup detail. They are most valuable when construction has branching logic, required sequencing, or expensive shared resources.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.excerpt: Structural design patterns organize how objects and interfaces fit together. They help engineers adapt incompatible APIs, compose nested structures, add behavior safely, and hide subsystem complexity.

nodes.label: Docker Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Docker packages applications into images and runs them as isolated containers. Its practical value is consistent development, CI, and deployment environments, but production use requires attention to layers, networking, volumes, and security.

nodes.label: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
nodes.excerpt: Domain-Driven Design is an approach to modeling software around the language, rules, and boundaries of the business domain. Its most useful ideas are ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, and keeping domain logic explicit.

nodes.label: Event Sourcing
nodes.excerpt: Event sourcing stores every state-changing event as the source of truth instead of only storing the latest state. It enables auditability and temporal reconstruction, but requires careful event schema, replay, and operational discipline.

nodes.label: Event-Driven Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Event-driven architecture coordinates systems through published facts about things that happened. It decouples producers from consumers, but shifts complexity into event design, ordering, idempotency, and observability.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.label: Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.excerpt: Feature flags decouple deploying code from releasing behavior. Progressive rollout uses flags to expose changes gradually, measure impact, and quickly disable risky behavior.

nodes.label: Git Flow vs Trunk-Based
nodes.excerpt: Git Flow and trunk-based development are contrasting branching models. Git Flow emphasizes release branches and staged stabilization, while trunk-based development emphasizes frequent integration into main.

nodes.label: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
nodes.excerpt: Hexagonal architecture places domain logic at the center and connects external systems through ports and adapters. It helps keep business rules independent from frameworks, databases, and transport details.

nodes.label: Integration Testing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Integration testing verifies that multiple components work together across real boundaries such as databases, APIs, queues, and filesystems. It catches contract and configuration bugs that unit tests intentionally ignore.

nodes.label: Kubernetes Basics
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes is a container orchestration system that keeps declared application state running across a cluster. It manages scheduling, service discovery, rollouts, health checks, scaling, and recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.label: Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.excerpt: Load and performance testing measure how a system behaves under expected, peak, and stressful traffic. The goal is to find capacity limits, latency bottlenecks, and failure modes before users do.

nodes.label: Microservices Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Microservices architecture decomposes a system into independently deployable services aligned around business capabilities. It can improve team autonomy and scaling, but it introduces distributed systems complexity that must be justified.

nodes.label: Monolith vs Microservices Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: The monolith versus microservices decision is a tradeoff between simplicity and independent service autonomy. The right answer depends on team size, domain boundaries, deployment needs, and operational maturity.

nodes.label: Monorepo vs Polyrepo
nodes.excerpt: Monorepo and polyrepo strategies organize source code around different tradeoffs in coordination, tooling, ownership, and release independence. The best choice depends on team scale, dependency coupling, and build infrastructure maturity.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.label: Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.excerpt: Open source licenses define what others may do with code and what obligations they inherit. Engineers need licensing literacy because dependency choices can affect distribution, compliance, and business risk.

nodes.label: OpenAPI and API Documentation
nodes.excerpt: OpenAPI describes REST-like HTTP APIs in a machine-readable contract. Good API documentation combines accurate schemas with human guidance, examples, error behavior, authentication, and compatibility notes.

nodes.label: Property-Based Testing
nodes.excerpt: Property-based testing checks general invariants over many generated inputs instead of only testing hand-picked examples. It is strongest for parsers, serializers, state machines, algorithms, and code with clear mathematical or domain properties.

nodes.label: Refactoring Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Refactoring is the disciplined process of improving code structure without changing observable behavior. Good refactoring is incremental, test-backed, and focused on reducing the next change's cost.

nodes.label: SOLID Principles
nodes.excerpt: SOLID is a set of object-oriented design principles for keeping classes and modules focused, replaceable, and easier to change. It is useful as a review lens, but harmful when applied mechanically without regard for simpler designs.

nodes.label: Semantic Versioning
nodes.excerpt: Semantic Versioning is a version numbering convention that communicates compatibility expectations through MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH numbers. It helps package users understand whether an update should be safe, additive, or breaking.

nodes.label: Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)
nodes.excerpt: A service mesh adds a dedicated infrastructure layer for service-to-service traffic management, observability, security, and policy. It is powerful in large microservice environments but can be unnecessary operational complexity for simpler systems.

nodes.label: Software Estimation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Software estimation is the practice of forecasting effort, uncertainty, and delivery risk. Good estimates communicate assumptions and ranges, not false precision.

nodes.label: Static Analysis Tools
nodes.excerpt: Static analysis tools inspect code without running it to find bugs, style issues, security risks, type errors, and maintainability problems. They provide cheap automated feedback when configured to match team priorities.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.label: Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.excerpt: Technical debt is the future cost created by implementation choices that make later change harder. Managing it means distinguishing deliberate tradeoffs from accidental decay and paying down the debt that blocks important work.

nodes.label: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
nodes.excerpt: Test-Driven Development is a workflow where tests are written before implementation to clarify desired behavior and guide design. Its value is fastest feedback and design pressure, not simply having more tests.

nodes.label: The Unix Philosophy
nodes.excerpt: The Unix philosophy favors small composable programs that do one thing well and communicate through simple interfaces. Its lasting lesson is that good software tools become powerful when they compose cleanly.

nodes.label: Trunk-Based Development
nodes.excerpt: Trunk-based development is a source-control workflow where engineers integrate small changes into the main branch frequently. It supports continuous integration by reducing long-lived branch drift.

nodes.label: Virtualization — VMs vs Containers
nodes.excerpt: Virtual machines and containers isolate workloads at different layers. VMs virtualize hardware with separate guest operating systems, while containers isolate processes that share the host kernel.

nodes.label: System Design Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: end-to-end design studies for feeds, chat, file sync, collaborative docs, dispatch, payments, video platforms, and job recommendations.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Rubric
nodes.excerpt: 8-phase rubric for FAANG-level system design interviews: requirements, scale estimation, API design, data model, architecture, deep dive, cross-cutting concerns, and evolution.

nodes.label: System Design Notes
nodes.excerpt: scalability, caching, SQL vs NoSQL, queues, load balancing, CAP theorem, rate limiting.

nodes.label: Bulkhead Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The bulkhead pattern isolates resources so one failing dependency or workload cannot exhaust the entire system. It is inspired by ship compartments that prevent one leak from sinking everything.

nodes.label: CAP Theorem
nodes.excerpt: CAP theorem says that during a network partition, a distributed data system must choose between consistency and availability. It is a partition-time tradeoff, not a general excuse for simplistic database labels.

nodes.label: CDN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: A content delivery network caches and serves content from edge locations close to users. CDNs improve latency, absorb traffic spikes, reduce origin load, and add security controls.

nodes.label: Cache Invalidation Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Cache invalidation is the process of removing or updating stale cached data. The right pattern depends on freshness requirements, write frequency, ownership, and tolerance for temporary inconsistency.

nodes.label: Caching Strategies (LRU, LFU, TTL)
nodes.excerpt: Caching stores frequently used data closer to callers to reduce latency, cost, and backend load. LRU, LFU, and TTL are common policies for deciding what stays fresh and what gets evicted.

nodes.label: Circuit Breaker Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The circuit breaker pattern stops calls to a failing dependency before failures cascade. It moves between closed, open, and half-open states based on recent success or failure.

nodes.label: Consensus Algorithms (Raft, Paxos)
nodes.excerpt: Consensus algorithms let distributed nodes agree on a value or ordered log despite failures. Raft and Paxos are foundational protocols for replicated state machines and strongly consistent coordination.

nodes.label: Consistent Hashing
nodes.excerpt: Consistent hashing distributes keys across changing sets of nodes while minimizing remapping. It is useful for caches, sharded storage, load distribution, and systems where nodes frequently join or leave.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.label: Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.excerpt: Content delivery and edge computing move data and computation closer to users. They reduce latency, origin load, and bandwidth costs while introducing cache, consistency, deployment, and observability tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Replication
nodes.excerpt: Database replication copies data from one database node to others for read scaling, high availability, disaster recovery, or geographic locality. Replication design determines consistency, failover behavior, and data-loss risk.

nodes.label: Database Sharding
nodes.excerpt: Database sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple nodes. It increases capacity but introduces routing, resharding, hotspot, transaction, and query-complexity tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Design Twitter X Feed
nodes.excerpt: Designing a Twitter/X feed tests fanout, timeline generation, ranking, social graph storage, media handling, caching, and realtime updates. The core tradeoff is fanout-on-write versus fanout-on-read.

nodes.label: Design Uber Ride-Sharing Backend
nodes.excerpt: Designing an Uber-like backend tests geospatial indexing, realtime location updates, matching, dispatch, pricing, trip state, payments, and reliability. It is a stateful realtime marketplace problem.

nodes.label: Design a Chat Application
nodes.excerpt: Designing a chat application tests realtime delivery, message storage, ordering, presence, fanout, read receipts, offline sync, and notification behavior. It combines realtime systems with durable data modeling.

nodes.label: Design a Distributed Cache
nodes.excerpt: Designing a distributed cache tests key partitioning, replication, eviction, consistency, failure recovery, hot keys, and client routing. It is a core infrastructure system for reducing backend load.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.label: Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.excerpt: Designing an S3-like file storage system tests object APIs, metadata, chunking, durability, replication, consistency, access control, and lifecycle management. The main challenge is cheap durable storage at massive scale.

nodes.label: Design a Notification System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a notification system tests event ingestion, user preferences, aggregation, channel delivery, idempotency, retries, rate limits, and fatigue controls. It separates social/product notifications from transactional reliability needs.

nodes.label: Design a Payment System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a payment system tests correctness, idempotency, ledger modeling, provider integration, retries, reconciliation, fraud checks, and auditability. Reliability matters more than raw throughput.

nodes.label: Design a Rate Limiter
nodes.excerpt: Designing a rate limiter tests algorithms, distributed counters, identity keys, storage, latency, consistency, and failure behavior. The goal is to protect systems while being predictable for legitimate clients.

nodes.label: Design a Recommendation System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a recommendation system tests candidate generation, ranking, feedback loops, feature pipelines, online serving, evaluation, and exploration. The architecture connects ML with product metrics.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.label: Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.excerpt: Designing a Calendly-like scheduling system tests availability calculation, calendar integrations, time zones, booking holds, conflict prevention, notifications, and consistency. Time handling is the hidden complexity.

nodes.label: Design a Search Engine
nodes.excerpt: Designing a search engine tests crawling or ingestion, indexing, ranking, query processing, freshness, autocomplete, and relevance evaluation. Search systems combine data pipelines with low-latency serving.

nodes.label: Design a URL Shortener
nodes.excerpt: Designing a URL shortener tests API design, key generation, redirects, storage, caching, analytics, abuse prevention, and high-read scalability. It is a classic compact system design problem.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.label: Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.excerpt: Designing a video streaming platform tests upload, transcoding, storage, CDN delivery, adaptive bitrate streaming, metadata, recommendations, and analytics. It is a media-heavy system with extreme bandwidth concerns.

nodes.label: Design an API Gateway
nodes.excerpt: Designing an API gateway tests request routing, authentication, authorization, rate limiting, load balancing, observability, protocol translation, and failure handling. It is the front door for many service architectures.

nodes.label: Distributed Locking
nodes.excerpt: Distributed locking coordinates exclusive access to shared resources across multiple processes or machines. It is useful but risky because lock correctness depends on timeouts, ownership, failures, and the underlying consistency model.

nodes.label: Distributed Transactions (2PC, Saga Pattern)
nodes.excerpt: Distributed transactions coordinate changes across multiple services or databases. Two-phase commit preserves atomicity through coordination, while sagas use local transactions and compensating actions.

nodes.label: Eventual Consistency
nodes.excerpt: Eventual consistency means replicas may temporarily disagree but will converge if no new updates occur. It improves availability and latency at the cost of stale reads and conflict handling.

nodes.label: Idempotency in Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: Idempotency means an operation can be applied multiple times with the same intended effect as applying it once. It is essential for safe retries, duplicate messages, and unreliable networks.

nodes.label: Leader Election
nodes.excerpt: Leader election is the process of selecting one node to coordinate work in a distributed system. It is used for primary replicas, schedulers, singleton jobs, and consensus groups.

nodes.label: Load Balancing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple servers to improve capacity, availability, and latency. Strategy choices depend on protocol, health checks, statefulness, geography, and failure behavior.

nodes.label: Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS)
nodes.excerpt: Message queues decouple producers and consumers by storing work or events between them. Kafka, RabbitMQ, and SQS represent different tradeoffs in ordering, durability, throughput, routing, and operations.

nodes.label: PACELC Theorem
nodes.excerpt: PACELC extends CAP by saying distributed systems trade consistency for availability during partitions, else trade latency for consistency during normal operation. It is often more useful than CAP for real architecture decisions.

nodes.label: Pub Sub Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Publish/subscribe architecture lets producers publish messages to topics without knowing which consumers receive them. It is useful for fanout, event-driven integration, and decoupled workflows.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.label: Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.excerpt: Rate limiting controls how many requests a user, client, or system can make in a period. Token bucket, leaky bucket, and sliding-window algorithms trade burst tolerance, smoothness, precision, and storage cost.

nodes.label: Real-Time Systems Design
nodes.excerpt: Real-time systems deliver updates with tight latency expectations. Design choices depend on latency budget, fanout, ordering, delivery guarantees, transport, backpressure, and failure handling.

nodes.label: Scalability — Horizontal vs Vertical
nodes.excerpt: Scalability is a system's ability to handle more load by adding resources or improving efficiency. Vertical scaling makes individual machines stronger, while horizontal scaling adds more machines.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Framework
nodes.excerpt: A system design interview framework gives a repeatable structure for turning vague product prompts into scalable architectures. It keeps candidates from jumping to boxes before clarifying requirements, scale, data, APIs, tradeoffs, and failure modes.

nodes.label: WebRTC Architecture
nodes.excerpt: WebRTC enables low-latency peer-to-peer audio, video, and data communication in browsers and apps. Its architecture combines signaling, ICE, STUN, TURN, RTP media, and optional SFU/MCU servers.

nodes.label: Testing Strategy
nodes.excerpt: testing strategy by layer and risk: unit, integration, E2E, contract, property, load, frontend/backend/database/LLM tests, mocks, flaky tests, and CI.

nodes.label: Transformer Architecture
nodes.excerpt: full transformer internals: tokenization, attention mechanism (Q/K/V), multi-head attention, residual stream, FFN, KV cache, modern improvements (RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU).

nodes.label: Vault Structure
nodes.excerpt: what each folder is for (Projects vs Areas vs Resources vs Maps vs Templates) and the PARA logic behind the structure.

nodes.label: WSL2 Setup
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 gotchas solved: PulseAudio audio fix, Windows host IP, Obsidian portproxy.

nodes.label: Web Animation
nodes.excerpt: CSS transitions/keyframes, GPU-only rule, GSAP, Framer Motion, React Spring, Lottie pipeline, Rive state machines, Three.js/R3F, what makes animations feel good (easing, stagger, spring physics, reduced motion).

nodes.label: What Is Obsidian
nodes.excerpt: what Obsidian is, what it can do, and the right workflow for this vault (discuss with AI first, then write clean notes).
